boundary: This verdict allows only the implemented OpenSSL scrypt new-root path, the explicit PBKDF2 legacy compatibility boundary, and the password/offline-guessing limitation.  It does not claim Argon2id deployment, hardware-backed credential release, full offline-attack resistance, or broader security readiness.

candidates.text: The per-file envelope is authenticated and block records are AEAD-protected, but the mount password remains the root credential.  The implementation has no hardware-backed credential release, audited rotation protocol, privileged-attacker defense, or side-channel evaluation.  File anchors are replayable.  TPM mode requires an administrator-provisioned index; retained probes cover fail-closed replay-after-advance and transient PCR-policy behavior, not persistent PCR-bound key release under provisioned TPM policy.

Envelope authentication before DEK use & \texttt{metadata\_load()} verifies the HMAC-protected envelope before \texttt{pqc\_open()} installs file state & Clean-remount regression and \texttt{user.pqc\_metadata} tamper rejection returning \texttt{EKEYREJECTED} & Remountable authenticated envelope; no hardware-bound credential release\\

The password-derived mount key is never hardware-released; scrypt does not remove weak-password or offline-guessing risk.  There is no hardware-backed credential release path for the mount key.  The rekey is open-file DEK refresh rather than deployed credential rotation: HMAC envelope rewrap for open files; runtime key epoch is in-memory bookkeeping, not a persistent anti-rollback journal.  There is no transactional rewrap recovery claim, and password-derived envelope alone is not rollback resistance; stale old envelopes fail only when generation/checkpoint/external-anchor evidence rejects them.

The evaluated key-plane workflow applies this rule by batching open-file descriptors, encapsulating on the selected executor, installing 32-byte refreshed DEK material, and rewriting the HMAC-authenticated envelope.  It is not a persistent KEM hierarchy or hardware-backed credential lifecycle.

The retained mounted ML-KEM-768 key-plane workflow opens 1,024 encrypted files and refreshes their HMAC-authenticated envelopes.  CPU-only refresh takes 24.399~ms (41,969.6 files/s).  With explicit slack, admission selects the GPU and finishes in 21.070~ms (48,599.0 files/s), a 1.158$\times$ speedup.  With zero slack and high memory-pressure telemetry, admission falls back to CPU in 24.411~ms with an AI-QoS-exhausted deferral.  This supports only maintenance-visible open-file envelope refresh, not hardware-backed credential release, a persistent KEM hierarchy, or foreground AI QoS recovery.
envelope refresh, not hardware credential release, persistent PCR binding, or

- `artifacts/validation/mount_key_lifecycle/`: source/artifact/paper gate manifest for the mount-key lifecycle boundary; it supports authenticated storage-format correctness and mounted open-file envelope refresh, while scoping out hardware-backed credential release, deployed credential rotation, persistent epoch anti-rollback journaling, transactional rewrap recovery, and credential-only rollback resistance

verdict.next_production_step: Move to D2-S0: add mounted-path crypto-plane route evidence so data-plane AES-GCM and key-plane PQC remain visibly separated.
verdict.smallest_safe_change: Keep the versioned KDF metadata record and derive the mount key from per-filesystem salt before making any stronger security claim.
